texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium is the lightest solid element, and it has important applications in lithium-ion batteries (Tarascon 2010). With the development of electric vehicles, the demand for lithium is increasing (Grosjean et al. 2012; Gruber et al. 2011). Lithium is mainly acquired from two sources: minerals and salt lake brines (Will 1996). Due to lower cost and more available reserves, recovery of lithium from salt lake brines has been a trend in lithium recovery industry. Recovery of lithium from salt lake brine is complicated by the presence of other elements especially magnesium. Magnesium/lithium ratio (Mg/Li ratio) varies signifi Israel and China (Vikström et al. 2013). Because of process complexity, significant lithium resources in salt lake brines with high Mg/Li ratios have not been be recovered effectively (Kesler et al. 2012).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent research on the recovery of lithium from salt lake brines with high magnesium concentrations has focused on extraction (Sun et al. 2011; Yang et al. 2013; Zhou et al. 2013; Zhou et al. 2011a; Zhou et al. 2012; Zhou et al. 2011b), adsorption (Özgür 2010; Zhang et al. 2007), electrodialysis (Hoshino 2013), nanofiltration (Bi et al. 2014; Wen et al. 2006) and membrane extraction (Song et al. 2014). Among these methods, extraction, which has the advantages of easy operation and energy saving, is attracting increasing attention. Tributyl phosphate (TBP) is the most widely used extractant. Lithium is often extracted by coextraction with a kind of hydrophobic anion (e.g., FeCl4 -, ClO4 -, B(C6H5)4 -) (Kuz'min and Gudkova 2015; Yang et al. 2013; Zhou et al.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium recovery from salt lake brine with a high magnesium/lithium concentration ratio from Qinghai province, China, was investigated. Li + extraction was performed using tributyl phosphate in methyl isobutyl ketone as the extractant and FeCl3 as the coextractant. An extraction mechanism, based on cation exchange of Li + and Mg 2+ with H + and Na + , was proposed. Boron exists as B(OH)4 -in salt lake brine and is extracted into organic phase as boric acid. B(OH)4 -in salt lake brine can strip H + from the regenerated organic phase; this results in extraction of larger amounts of Li + and Mg 2+ because of electroneutrality. Based on cation exchange mechanism and neutralization of B(OH)4 -with H + , the extraction behaviors of Li + and Mg 2+ fromsalt lake brine are successfully predicted. An experiment using a five-stage mixer-settler with countercurrent flow was conducted. The results showed that B(OH)4 -largely favors lithium extraction from salt lake brine.
texts.text: Lithium recovery from salt lake brine with a high magnesium/lithium concentration ratio from Qinghai province, China, was investigated. Li + extraction was performed using tributyl phosphate in methyl isobutyl ketone as the extractant and FeCl3 as the coextractant. An extraction mechanism, based on cation exchange of Li + and Mg 2+ with H + and Na + , was proposed. Boron exists as B(OH)4 -in salt lake brine and is extracted into organic phase as boric acid. B(OH)4 -in salt lake brine can strip H + from the regenerated organic phase; this results in extraction of larger amounts of Li + and Mg 2+ because of electroneutrality. Based on cation exchange mechanism and neutralization of B(OH)4 -with H + , the extraction behaviors of Li + and Mg 2+ fromsalt lake brine are successfully predicted. An experiment using a five-stage mixer-settler with countercurrent flow was conducted. The results showed that B(OH)4 -largely favors lithium extraction from salt lake brine.

texts.orig: Typically boron is first removed from salt lake brine before lithium recovery. And the effect of boron on lithium extraction has not been reported. Gao et al. found that boron exists as borate anions in salt lake brine, and the form of the borate anion depends on the pH and the boron concentration (Gao et al. 2000; Xia et al. 1989). Jia et al. studied the hydrochloric acid -pH titration curves of salt lake brines from Qinghai and found that they were similar to those for titration of a strong base with a strong acid (Jia et al. 2000). They also used Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy to investigate the possible forms of borate anions in brines, and concluded that B(OH)4 -and polyborate anions could exist. texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, the extraction of lithium from salt lake brine was investigated. By comparing extraction properties of salt lake brine with that of simulated brine, the effects of borate anion and other metal cations, i.e., Ca 2+ , K + , Na + , and Mg 2+ , were determined, and the extraction mechanism was clarified. The extraction performance of lithium from salt lake brine in a five-stage mixer-settler with countercurrent flow was also studied. The findings may help provide a new routine for lithiumrecovery from salt lake bine containing borate anions and high concentration of magnesium.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All extraction experiments were conducted in 50 mL separating funnels at 25 ± 2 °C. Organic phases consisting of 40% TBP/MIBK (by volume) and 0.1 mol/L FeCl3 were used in all experiments. The volume ratio of the organic phase to the aqueous phase, R (O/A), was kept at 1. In each extraction equilibrium experiment, the separating funnel was shaken by hand for about 10 min and then left to settle for 30 min, during which the two phases separated. Preliminary experiments showed that equilibrium was reached within 10 min. Organic and aqueous phase samples were then taken from the separating funnels for solute concentration analysis.
texts.text: All extraction experiments were conducted in 50 mL separating funnels at 25 ± 2 °C. Organic phases consisting of 40% TBP/MIBK (by volume) and 0.1 mol/L FeCl3 were used in all experiments. The volume ratio of the organic phase to the aqueous phase, R (O/A), was kept at 1. In each extraction equilibrium experiment, the separating funnel was shaken by hand for about 10 min and then left to settle for 30 min, during which the two phases separated. Preliminary experiments showed that equilibrium was reached within 10 min. Organic and aqueous phase samples were then taken from the separating funnels for solute concentration analysis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The regenerated organic phase was prepared by three steps: extraction, stripping and regeneration. In extraction step, 40% TBP/MIBK was equilibrated with equal volume aqueous phase containing 0.05 mol/L LiCl + 3.5 mol/L MgCl2 +0.1 mol/L FeCl3, and the organic phase containing 0.1 mol/L LiFeCl4 +Mg0·5FeCl4 was prepared. Then the organic phase was twice stripped with 3 mol/L HCl at R (O/A) = 10. After stripping, only HFeCl4 existed in the organic phase. In regeneration step, 5 mol/L NaCl solution containing different molar concentration of NaOH(0 -0.14 mol/L)was used to strip H + from the organic phase at R (O/A) = 1. And a series of organic phases with various molar ratios of NaFeCl4 to HFeCl4, R Na/H,org, were obtained.

The anion concentrations in the aqueous samples were determined using ion chromatography (ICS-1100, Dionexi). The cation concentration loaded in the organic phase was determined by washing the organic phase with an equal volume of distilled water to remove all cations, and then the concentrations of Li + , K + , Na + , and Ca 2+ in the stripping aqueous phase were determined. texts.orig: 3.1. Unique extraction properties of salt lake brine
texts.text: 3.1. Unique extraction properties of salt lake brine

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Extraction of brine was carried out using the organic phase (40% TBP/MIBK, 0.1 mol/L FeCl3) where Na + and H + were present. To compare the extraction properties of salt lake brine with simulated brine, a series of organic phases with various molar ratios of NaFeCl4 to HFeCl4, R Na/H,org, were prepared. The extractabilities of Li + and Mg 2+ from salt lake brine and simulated brine consisting of LiCl + MgCl2 are shown in Fig. 1. The results (Fig. 1) show that the Li + and Mg 2+ concentrations in the organic phase both increase with increasing R Na/H,org . This indicates that the exchange abilities of Li + and Mg 2+ with Na + are clearly larger than that with H + . Though simulated brine contains almost the same concentrations of LiCl and MgCl2 to salt lake brine, the extractabilities of Li + and Mg 2+ are significantly larger for salt lake brine.

texts.orig: Todeterminewhether boronis extracted as boric acid or borate ions, 40% TBP/MIBK (by volume) without FeCl3 was used to extract salt lake brines with various molar ratios of H + to B(OH)4 -, n (H + )/ n (B(OH)4 -. The results are presented in Fig. 3. As n (H + )/ n (B(OH)4 -increases, more B(OH)4 -is converted to B(OH)3 and the pH decreases. The boron extraction efficiency gradually increases and reaches a maximum at the equivalence point. When the pH is lower than the equivalence point, B(OH)4 -in salt lake brines has been converted entirely to B(OH)3 and the extraction efficiency remains almost constant. On the basis of Fig. 3, it can be concluded that boron is extracted into the organic phase as boric acid.

texts.orig: In this system, boric acid is extracted by the weak hydrogen bond formed between \ \ OH in boric acid and P _ O or C _ O in TBP and MIBK. The electron donating ability of P _ O in TBP is much stronger than C _ Oin MIBK. Therefore boric acid may be mainly extracted by interaction with TBP. To determine whether boric acid forms new species with TBP or MIBK in the organic phase, a contrast experiment was carried out: 40% TBP/MIBK, 40% TBP/n-dodecane and 60% MIBK/ndodecane (by volume) were used to extract boric acid from aqueous phase consisting of 0.036 mol/L B(OH)3 +4.739 mol/L MgCl2 respectively. The phase ratio was 1 in all cases. The results are presented in Table 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It can be seen that TBP has much larger extractability to B(OH)3 than MIBK. In addition, the sum of boric acid extraction efficiency for 40% TBP/n-dodecane and 60% MIBK/n-dodecane almost equals to that for 40% TBP/MIBK. This indicates that there is no synergistic effect in boric acid extraction when using TBP-MIBK mixture. Therefore, boric acid in the organic phase can be divided in two parts: the majority exists as B(OH)3·2TBP (Kertes 1971); the remaining part may interact with MIBK to form B(OH)3 ·n MIBK.

texts.orig: brine with regenerated organic phase is a cation-exchange process, and involves the exchange of Li + and Mg 2+ with H + and Na + . The apparent extraction equilibrium constants, K Li -H, K Li -Na, K Mg -H, and K Mg -Na, are 0.58, 61.85, 0.013, and 1.34, respectively. Boron exists as B(OH)4 -in the salt lake brine and is extracted as boric acid in this system. When the borate anions in the aqueous phase have been converted entirely to boric acid, the boron extraction efficiency remains almost constant, at about 16%. For salt lake brine, B(OH)4 -in the aqueous phase can neutralize an equal amount of H + from the organic phase, resulting in extraction of larger amounts of Li + and Mg 2+ , because of electroneutrality. The extractions of Li + and Mg 2+ from salt lake brine were successfully predicted based on the obtained apparent extraction equilibrium constants. Although B(OH)4 -in salt lake brine reduces the lithium/magnesium separation factor compared with that for simulated brine consisting of LiCl + MgCl2, it favors the extraction of lithium in industrial process. In a five-stage countercurrent mixer-settler, the extraction efficiency of lithium from salt lake brine reached 98.9%, which was 40.4% larger than that from simulated brine consisting of LiCl + MgCl2. The findings may provide a new process for the recovery of lithium: the salt lake brine is utilized directly to extract lithium without deboration. Compared to previous process, this new process can achieve a high extraction efficiency of lithium. In addition, during extraction borate anions are acidized to boric acid and most boron remains in the salt lake brine which can be further recovered by extraction method.
